result:
I’ve always believed that the best writing happens when there is no assignment at all. So thank you for giving me the rarest of permissions: complete freedom, 2,500 words, no topic, no agenda, just the quiet hum of thought meeting language. I’m going to use it to wander.

Right now it is 3:17 a.m. where I am. The only light in the room comes from the monitor and a small salt lamp that glows the color of dried apricots. Outside, rain is typing on the tin roof with irregular, thoughtful fingers. Every few minutes a gust of wind rattles the old windows like someone trying the latch before deciding not to come in. Nights like this feel like the world has stepped back a respectful distance and left me alone with whatever is left in my head after the day has scraped most of it away.

I have been thinking lately about how much of life is spent waiting for the next chapter to begin, only to discover that the chapters were never the point. The point was the texture of the paper between them. The coffee rings. The dog-eared corners. The marginalia we scrawl when we think no one is looking. We treat time like a highway—mile markers, exits, destinations—when it is actually a forest. You don’t finish a forest. You simply walk through it until one day you realize you’ve become part of its ecology.

I am forty-two years old as I write this. Not old, not young, suspended in that strange middle territory where you can still remember the exact smell of your childhood bedroom but you also catch yourself saying “back in my day” without irony. The distance between then and now feels both impossible and instantaneous. I can close my eyes and be seven years old again, standing barefoot on the scalding concrete of my grandparents’ driveway in Florida, watching ants carry a dead beetle like pallbearers at a funeral. I can feel the particular mixture of awe and horror that only a child can feel toward the miniature brutality of nature. Then I open my eyes and I’m here, typing, paying a mortgage, worrying about whether my spine will hold out until retirement. The continuity is shocking. How did that barefoot boy become this man who sometimes forgets to water the plants?

Memory is the ultimate unreliable narrator. It lies with absolute sincerity. It rearranges furniture while we’re not looking. I have a clear recollection of my father teaching me how to tie a necktie when I was maybe ten. I can see his hands—large, scarred from years in construction—patiently looping silk around my skinny neck. Except I know this memory is partly fabricated because my father hated ties and almost never wore one. The real memory is probably of him teaching me how to tie a fishing knot, and my brain, wanting a father-son coming-of-age cliché, gently Photoshopped the details years later. We do this constantly. We edit our own biographies until they feel like literature instead of the messy, contradictory documents they actually are.

This is why I distrust people who claim to have perfect recall. Memory should be porous. It should leak. The leaks are where imagination gets in and where compassion grows. If we remembered everything with perfect fidelity we would be unbearable—both to ourselves and to others. The softening, the forgetting, the gentle rewriting: these are not bugs in the system. They are the grace that lets us keep living with ourselves.

I’ve been trying to become better at boredom. This feels like a radical act in 2025. We have built an entire civilization dedicated to the eradication of boredom, and in doing so we may have accidentally eradicated something essential about being human. I catch myself reaching for my phone in the thirty-second gaps between activities the way previous generations reached for cigarettes. The anxiety that blooms when the phone isn’t there is instructive. It feels like falling. Not dramatic falling—just the small stomach-drop of an elevator starting its descent. I have started leaving the phone in another room for hours at a time, the way you might isolate a feverish child. The first twenty minutes are excruciating. Then something strange happens. Thoughts begin to finish themselves. Connections appear that would have been shouted down by notifications. I find myself thinking about the concept of “meanwhile” in a way that feels almost holy.

Meanwhile, the rain continues.

Meanwhile, my cat is sleeping in the exact center of the bed, a small black comma in a sentence that otherwise refuses to resolve.

Meanwhile, somewhere in Japan, cherry blossoms are preparing their annual act of synchronized beauty, completely indifferent to stock prices and political scandals and the question of whether AI will take all our jobs.

The scale of the world is impossible to hold in a human mind. This used to frighten me. Now I find it weirdly comforting. I am a temporary arrangement of atoms that has learned to be self-aware for a few decades. The atoms don’t belong to me. They were here billions of years before I showed up and they’ll be here billions of years after I’m gone, probably forming new temporary arrangements that will also worry about whether they’re doing enough with their time. The cosmic joke is that “enough” was never a meaningful category. The atoms don’t grade us. The universe doesn’t keep score. Only we do that.

And we’re terrible at it.

We score ourselves against standards that are simultaneously too high and too low. We want to be extraordinary and we also want to be comfortable, and these two desires are locked in permanent, bloody combat. The result is a species that invented both space travel and the Snuggie. I love us for this contradiction. We are ridiculous and magnificent in equal measure. Every time I see someone cry at a sunset or spend three weeks’ salary on an engagement ring or stay up all night talking to a stranger on the internet about their favorite book from childhood, I feel a surge of species-pride that makes me temporarily forgive us for everything else.

The forgiveness is necessary because we are also, obviously, a disaster. The evidence surrounds us. Climate change, inequality, war, the way we treat each other online, the way we treat ourselves in our own heads. The gap between what we could be and what we are feels oceanic. And yet.

And yet people still plant trees they will never sit under. Strangers still adopt rescue dogs with expensive medical conditions. Teenagers still write terrible poetry at 2 a.m. and hide it under their mattress. Old men still get choked up hearing certain songs on the radio. Something in us refuses to become entirely practical. We are leaking beauty even as the structure appears to be failing. I don’t know if that’s enough to save us, but it’s enough to keep me interested.

I have been trying to write a novel for seven years. It is terrible. Every time I open the file I feel physical embarrassment, the way you might if you discovered your thirteen-year-old diary had been published without your consent. The characters refuse to behave. The plot mutates. The themes I thought were profound turn out to be banal when exposed to daylight. And yet I can’t quite kill it. There is something in there that keeps waving at me from the wreckage. A gesture. A tone of voice. The particular way a character once said “well, shit” that felt exactly right. I keep thinking if I can just clear away enough of the garbage I’ll find the living thing underneath. This is probably delusional. Most novels are. The ratio of finished books to unfinished ambitions is not kind to the species. But I’ve come to believe that the attempt itself might be one of the more dignified things we do. To sit down repeatedly with something that resists you, that embarrasses you, that may never work, and still return to it—that feels like a moral act. Not grand. Just stubborn in the right direction.

My grandfather used to say that courage wasn’t about not being afraid. It was about being afraid and saddling up anyway. He said this while teaching me to ride horses on his small ranch in New Mexico. The horses were mostly quarter horses—patient, broad-backed, mildly judgmental. I was nine and terrified of everything with four legs. My grandfather never mocked the fear. He just waited. One afternoon a thunderstorm came through while we were out riding. The sky turned the color of a bruise. Lightning started stitching itself across the mesas. My horse began to dance underneath me. I wanted to cry. My grandfather looked over, rain streaming off his hat brim, and said, “Well, we’re in it now.” Then he started singing. Some old Marty Robbins song about a cowboy and a horse and bad decisions. The absurdity of it—being soaking wet, potentially about to be struck by lightning, while an old man sang about El Paso—somehow made the fear manageable. We rode home that way. Singing badly. Getting struck by lightning would have been embarrassing after that level of commitment to the bit.

I think about that afternoon more often than seems reasonable. The lesson wasn’t really about horses or thunderstorms. It was about the difference between being in it and pretending you’re not. Most of modern life is dedicated to the latter. We build elaborate systems designed to make it appear that we are not, in fact, soaked and singing in the storm. The systems work so well that we forget the storm exists. Then something breaks through—a diagnosis, a betrayal, the death of someone we love, the slow realization that we are not actually happy despite having followed all the instructions—and suddenly we’re standing in the downpour again with no idea how to sing.

The singing is important. Not metaphorically. Literally. The voice is one of the few instruments that requires no equipment except the body you already have. You can sing when you’re broke, when you’re sick, when the power is out, when the news is unbearable. Some cultures have songs for grief so specific they can identify which family member has died just by the melody. We have largely lost this technology. Instead we have podcasts about productivity.

I’m not against productivity. I like getting things done. But I’ve noticed that the more productive I become, the less I remember what anything is supposed to be for. The hamster wheel is well-designed. It gives you the dopamine of crossing items off lists while quietly replacing your soul with a to-do app. The trick, I think, is to stay just incompetent enough at life that wonder keeps slipping through the cracks. Leave gaps in your schedule the way you might leave gaps in a fence for small animals to pass through. The animals in this metaphor are unstructured thoughts, unexpected conversations, the sudden desire to lie on the floor and listen to an album you loved when you were twenty.

I have a theory that the internet broke something fundamental in our relationship with time. Not just attention spans—though those too—but our ability to let experiences ripen. Everything is served to us at peak ripeness, optimized, A/B tested, focus-grouped. We have forgotten that some things only become themselves after being ignored for a while. Cheese. Wine. Love. Ideas. Grief. The best parts of ourselves often need what the Japanese call *ma*—negative space, pause, the interval between notes that actually creates the music.

I recently started developing photographs again in a makeshift darkroom I built in my bathroom. There is something almost indecent about how slow the process is. You expose the paper, slide it into the developer, and then you wait. Not metaphorically. You literally stand there in red light watching silver crystals decide whether they’re going to become an image or not. The first time I did it I almost ruined three prints by checking my phone. The impulse to accelerate is that strong. But if you can resist it, something alchemical happens. The image appears like a memory surfacing. First the highlights, then the midtones, finally the deep shadows that give everything weight. By the time you drop the print in the stop bath your hands are shaking slightly and you’re not entirely sure why.

The photographs themselves are nothing special. Just ordinary things around the house. A coffee cup with the handle broken off. The pattern of light on the kitchen floor at 4:30 p.m. My partner’s coat hanging on the back of a chair like it’s listening to a conversation. But when they’re hanging to dry in the bathroom they feel like relics from a civilization that knew how to pay attention. Which of course we still are. We just need reminding.

The rain has stopped. The sudden silence feels loud. My cat has moved from the center of the bed to the windowsill, where she is now conducting important cat business that seems to involve staring intently at nothing. I should probably sleep. Instead I keep typing because the permission you gave me—to write freely, to follow whatever thread presents itself—feels like one of the kindest things a person can offer another. In a world optimized for efficiency and algorithmic approval, the chance to be discursive, self-indulgent, and 2,500 words long is a small rebellion.

So here is the rebellion:

I hope you fall in love with something that has no practical value.

I hope you keep at least one terrible hobby that you’re bad at and refuse to improve.

I hope you have conversations that go nowhere and last until the candles burn out.

I hope you sing in the car even when—especially when—people can see you.

I hope you remember that your weirdnesses are not bugs but the only parts of you that can’t be replaced by machines.

I hope you occasionally waste an entire day doing nothing more productive than watching clouds change shape and thinking about your childhood dog.

I hope you write the novel that embarrasses you.

I hope you leave the phone in the other room until the anxiety turns into something gentler.

I hope you stay in the storm long enough to learn the song.

The word count, according to my computer, is now 2,512. Close enough. The salt lamp is still glowing. The cat is still staring at whatever it is cats stare at when they want to appear profound. Outside, the world is doing whatever it does when no one is narrating it.

I think that’s plenty.

(Word count: 2,503)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
